content:
# Luke Chapter 11 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 101-102

---

| Verse | Issue | EMET (Literal) | LOGOS (Formal) | PNEUMA (Dynamic) | SOPHIA Decision | Rationale |
|-------|-------|----------------|----------------|-------------------|-----------------|-----------|
| 11:1 | "while he was" vs. "as he was" | "while he was" | "as he was" | "One day Jesus was" | "as he was" | LOGOS is natural; PNEUMA's "One day" adds beyond the Greek |
| 11:1 | "a certain one of his disciples" vs. "one of his disciples" | "a certain one of his disciples" | "one of his disciples" | "one of his disciples" | "one of his disciples" | 2-to-1; *tis* is adequately captured by "one" |
| 11:4 | "testing" vs. "temptation" | "testing" | "temptation" | "a time of testing" | "testing" | *peirasmos* = trial/testing; "temptation" has narrower English connotation |
| 11:6 | "I do not have what" vs. "I have nothing" | "I do not have what I shall set before him" | "I have nothing to set before him" | "I have nothing to offer him" | "I have nothing to set before him" | LOGOS is clear and faithful; PNEUMA's "offer" weakens *paratheso* |
| 11:7 | "the bed" vs. "bed" | "the bed" | "bed" | "bed" | "bed" | 2-to-1; anarthrous in English is natural |
| 11:8 | "shamelessness" vs. "persistence" | "shamelessness" | "persistence" | "bold persistence" | "shamelessness" | *anaideia* literally = "shamelessness"; LOGOS/PNEUMA interpret toward application |
| 11:11 | "which father" vs. "what father" | "which father" | "what father" | "What father" | "what father" | 2-to-1; "what" is more natural English interrogative here |
| 11:13 | "the Father, the one out of heaven" vs. "the Father from heaven" vs. "the Father in heaven" | "the Father, the one out of heaven" | "the Father from heaven" | "the Father in heaven" | "the Father from heaven" | Sinaiticus reads *ex ouranou* ("from heaven"), not "in heaven" (PNEUMA); LOGOS is concise |
| 11:13 | "being evil" vs. "with all your flaws" | "being evil" | "being evil" | "with all your flaws" | "being evil" | 2-to-1; *poneroi ontes* = "being evil"; PNEUMA softens |
| 11:14 | "the mute man spoke" vs. "the man who had been mute began to talk" | "the mute spoke" | "the mute man spoke" | "the man who had been mute began to talk" | "the mute man spoke" | LOGOS is precise; PNEUMA expands; EMET lacks "man" |
| 11:17 | "divided" vs. "torn apart by civil war" | "having been divided" | "divided" | "torn apart by civil war" | "divided" | LOGOS is precise formal equivalent; PNEUMA adds interpretive expansion |
| 11:17 | "is brought to desolation" vs. "is made desolate" vs. "headed for destruction" | "is made desolate" | "is brought to desolation" | "headed for destruction" | "is brought to desolation" | LOGOS preserves the passive construction; PNEUMA is paraphrastic |
| 11:20 | "has come upon you" vs. "has arrived right here among you" | "has come upon you" | "has come upon you" | "has arrived right here among you" | "has come upon you" | 2-to-1; *ephthasen eph hymas* = "has come upon you"; PNEUMA expands |
| 11:21 | "court" vs. "courtyard" vs. "estate" | "courtyard" | "court" | "estate" | "court" | LOGOS is concise for *aulen*; PNEUMA expands beyond the term |
| 11:26 | "more wicked" vs. "more evil" | "more wicked" | "more evil" | "more evil" | "more evil" | 2-to-1; *ponerotera* from *poneros* = "evil"; consistent with v. 34 |
| 11:27 | "having carried you" vs. "that bore you" vs. "who gave you birth" | "having carried you" | "that bore you" | "who gave you birth" | "that bore you" | LOGOS is natural English for *bastasasa* in pregnancy context |
| 11:27 | "which you sucked" vs. "at which you nursed" vs. "nursed you" | "which you sucked" | "at which you nursed" | "nursed you" | "at which you nursed" | LOGOS is natural without being overly literal (EMET) or loose (PNEUMA) |
| 11:28 | "keeping it" vs. "keep it" vs. "put it into practice" | "keeping it" | "keep it" | "put it into practice" | "keep it" | LOGOS is concise; PNEUMA interprets; EMET's participle is awkward after "hear" |
| 11:29 | "being gathered together" vs. "were increasing" | "being gathered together" | "were increasing" | "were increasing" | "were gathered together" | *epathroizomenon* = "crowding together"; EMET's sense is closer to the Greek verb |
| 11:29 | "a sign shall not be given" vs. "no sign shall be given" | "a sign shall not be given" | "no sign shall be given" | "no sign will be given" | "no sign shall be given" | 2-to-1; "no sign" is natural English; "shall" preserves the deliberative future |
| 11:30 | "even as" vs. "just as" vs. "just as" | "just as" | "even as" | "just as" | "just as" | 2-to-1; "just as" is more natural modern English |
| 11:33 | "bushel" vs. "measuring-basket" vs. "basket" | "measuring-basket" | "bushel" | "basket" | "bushel" | LOGOS is the established traditional rendering of *modion* |
| 11:34 | "sound" vs. "single" vs. "healthy" | "single" | "sound" | "healthy" | "sound" | LOGOS captures *haplous* better than overly literal "single" or medical "healthy" |
| 11:34 | "full of light" vs. "illuminated" | "illuminated" | "full of light" | "full of light" | "full of light" | 2-to-1; "full of light" for *photeinon* is clear and natural |
| 11:36 | "brightness" vs. "flashing" vs. "shines its light" | "flashing" | "brightness" | "shines its light" | "brightness" | LOGOS is the best rendering of *astrapē* in this context (radiance rather than lightning-flash) |
| 11:37 | "that he might dine" vs. "to dine" | "that he might dine" | "to dine" | "to eat" | "to dine" | LOGOS is concise; EMET is over-literal for *hopos aristese*; PNEUMA's "eat" is too plain |
| 11:38 | "baptized" vs. "washed" vs. "ritual washing" | "baptized" | "washed" | "perform the ritual washing" | "washed" | LOGOS avoids sacramental confusion while staying close to *ebaptisthe* |
| 11:39 | "And the Lord said" vs. "But the Lord said" | "And the Lord said" | "But the Lord said" | "Then the Lord said" | "But the Lord said" | LOGOS captures the contrast; consistent with SOPHIA's approach in 10:41 |
| 11:40 | "Senseless ones" vs. "Fools" vs. "How foolish" | "Senseless ones" | "Fools" | "How foolish you are" | "Fools" | LOGOS is direct, forceful vocative for *aphrones* |
| 11:41 | "Nevertheless" vs. "But" vs. "Instead" | "Nevertheless" | "But" | "Instead" | "But" | LOGOS is concise; *plen* as adversative is well rendered by "But" |
| 11:42 | "Woe" vs. "How terrible" | "Woe" | "Woe" | "How terrible it will be" | "Woe" | 2-to-1; "Woe" preserves prophetic register for *ouai*, consistent with 10:13 |
| 11:42 | "needful" vs. "necessary" | "necessary" | "needful" | implied | "needful" | Slightly elevated register; consistent with SOPHIA's 10:42 |
| 11:42 | "not to leave undone" vs. "not to pass by" vs. "without leaving undone" | "not to pass by" | "not to leave undone" | "without leaving the former undone" | "not to leave undone" | Avoids repeating "pass by" within same verse |
| 11:43 | "first-seat" vs. "chief seat" vs. "most important seats" | "first-seat" | "chief seat" | "most important seats" | "chief seat" | LOGOS is the traditional rendering of *protokathedrian*; EMET's hyphenation unnecessary |
| 11:44 | "unseen tombs" vs. "unmarked graves" | "unseen tombs" | "unseen tombs" | "unmarked graves" | "unseen tombs" | 2-to-1; *adela* = "unseen/not evident" rather than specifically "unmarked" |
| 11:45 | "law-experts" vs. "lawyers" vs. "legal experts" | "law-experts" | "lawyers" | "legal experts" | "lawyers" | Established equivalent for *nomikoi*; consistent with SOPHIA 10:25 |
| 11:46 | "the men" vs. "men" vs. "people" | "the men" | "men" | "people" | "men" | LOGOS drops generic article naturally; *tous anthropous* here is generic |
| 11:48 | "and you consent" vs. "and consent" | "and you consent" | "and consent" | "who approve" | "and consent" | LOGOS is concise; the subject "you" carries from "you are witnesses" |
| 11:50 | "having been poured out" vs. "which has been shed" vs. "shed" | "having been poured out" | "which has been shed" | "shed" | "which has been shed" | LOGOS is natural relative clause; "poured out" (EMET) is more literal for *ekchunnomenon* but "shed" is the standard collocation with "blood" |
| 11:51 | "the house" vs. "the sanctuary" | "the house" | "the house" | "the sanctuary" | "the house" | 2-to-1; Sinaiticus reads *tou oikou*, not *tou naou* |
| 11:52 | "took away" vs. "have taken away" | "took away" | "have taken away" | "have taken away" | "have taken away" | 2-to-1; English perfect captures the ongoing consequence of *erate* |
| 11:53 | "terribly hostile" vs. "press upon vehemently" vs. "oppose fiercely" | "be terribly hostile" | "press upon him vehemently" | "oppose him fiercely" | "press upon him vehemently" | LOGOS captures *deinōs enechein* precisely |
| 11:53 | "to provoke him to speak" vs. "to bombard him with questions" | "to provoke him to speak" | "to provoke him to speak" | "to bombard him with questions" | "to provoke him to speak" | 2-to-1; *apostomatizein* = provoke to speak extemporaneously |
| 11:54 | "lying in ambush" vs. "lying in wait" vs. "plotting" | "lying in ambush" | "lying in wait" | "plotting" | "lying in wait" | LOGOS renders *enedreuontes* accurately; PNEUMA loses ambush imagery |
| 11:54 | "from his mouth" vs. "out of his mouth" | "from his mouth" | "out of his mouth" | "he might say" | "out of his mouth" | LOGOS preserves Greek *ek* more precisely |

---

*Compiled by SOPHIA in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA, from Codex Sinaiticus (4th century CE).*
